//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
On Windows the workload identity is *mirrored across two namespaces*:
a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
WSL execution context entered by the forced command.
The Windows-user shape mirrors the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin});
the WSL-side `useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as
the named WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's
default user.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{burc_workload_user} in *both* namespaces and recreates it from a
clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user}
identity in *both* namespaces — the Windows user via
`net.exe user /delete` and the WSL Linux user via `userdel -r`.
Both purges are idempotent.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} in *both*
namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
/active:yes` (mirroring the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side
authorized_keys at `C:\Users\${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}\.ssh\authorized_keys`
(reachable from WSL as `/mnt/c/Users/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/...`)
so Windows OpenSSH discovers it natively. The c branch is the
structural reference for mkdir/chmod/echo/chmod/chown ordering.
The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` is substituted with the
configured workload user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{burc_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
workload validation failure.

21	by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac this
22	admin trust is operator-managed (e.g., placed via `ssh-copy-id`) —

29	//axhos_step
30	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
31	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
32	missing or stale admin trust.
33	
34	//axhos_step
35	. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} account
36	via `userdel -r`.
37	
38	//axhos_step
39	. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
40	ssh-only account.
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
44	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
45	the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
46	mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
47	+
48	[listing]
49	----
50	command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
51	----
52	
53	//axhos_step
54	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
55	account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
56	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
57	
58	//axhos_step
59	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
60	exit 0.
61	
62	//axhog_guarantee
63	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
64	{burc_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
65	outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.
66	
67	//axhoc_completion
68	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
69	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
70	failure, or workload validation failure.
71

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_bash}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
for Linux and macOS nodes.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{burc_workload_user}
and recreates it from a clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac this
admin trust is operator-managed (e.g., placed via `ssh-copy-id`) —
there is no `busn_fenestrate` for non-Windows-OpenSSH nodes.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} account
via `userdel -r`.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
ssh-only account.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
+
[listing]
----
command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload account named
{burc_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
failure, or workload validation failure.

Project-wide convention name for the unprivileged workload OS user that

provisions on every node — single source of truth, one workload user
per node.

Validation enforces a name-collision blacklist (against well-known
admin/service accounts) and platform-imposed length limits.

=== BURS Regime

profiles.

{burs_tincture}::
Per-station tincture composed by test fixtures into cloud/runtime prefixes
and family stems so parallel test runs from different stations land in

content.content:
1545	across station users (git-transported); it carries node shape only and
1546	no key material.
1547	A
1548	{burp_regime}
1549	instance lives under `.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/`;
1550	the
1551	{burs_user}
1552	owns its credential subtree exclusively, and operations write only
1553	under the current station user's tree.
1554	
1555	{busn_garrison}
1556	authenticates as
1557	{burp_privileged_user}
1558	using
1559	{burp_privileged_key_file}
1560	by key alone; admin trust is established beforehand by
1561	{busn_fenestrate}
1562	on Windows (which uses a one-time password fallback on its own
1563	phase-1 admin SSH session) or operator-manually on Linux/Mac.
1564	Workload account is unprivileged, ssh-only, and named by
1565	{burc_workload_user}
1566	(project-wide convention, single source of truth).
1567	
1568	=== Garrison-Destructive Model
1569	
1570	Every
1571	{busn_garrison}
1572	invocation wipes prior workload state on the node regardless of which
1573	{burp_investiture}
1574	or station user runs it.
1575	There is exactly one workload user per node, ever; node runtime state
1576	belongs to whoever last garrisoned it.
1577	Garrison subsumes the workload-account lifecycle: there is no separate
1578	verb to materialize, remove, or enumerate workload accounts — every
1579	garrison rebuilds from a clean slate.
1580	+
1581	Admin-side sshd configuration is out of garrison's scope.
1582	{busn_fenestrate}
1583	owns Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening; on Linux/Mac the
1584	operator manages sshd directly.
1585	
1586	=== Tabtarget Catalog
1587	
1588	Fifteen tabtargets cover regime configuration, fenestrate, privileged
1589	SSH, garrison ceremony, and workload dispatch.
1590	PowerShell garrison and dispatch are deferred and not enumerated below.
1591	
1592	==== Regime Configuration
1593	
1594	[[bust_node_regime_list]]
1595	//axvo_tabtarget
1596	{bust_node_regime_list}::
1597	`buw-rnl` —
1598	List all {burn_regime} profiles under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`.
1599	
1600	[[bust_node_regime_render]]
1601	//axvo_tabtarget
1602	{bust_node_regime_render}::
1603	`buw-rnr` —
1604	Render a single {burn_regime} profile
1605	({burn_investiture} passed as positional argument).
1606	
1607	[[bust_node_regime_validate]]
1608	//axvo_tabtarget
1609	{bust_node_regime_validate}::
1610	`buw-rnv` —
1611	Validate a single {burn_regime} profile
1612	({burn_investiture} passed as positional argument).
1613	
1614	[[bust_privilege_regime_list]]
1615	//axvo_tabtarget
1616	{bust_privilege_regime_list}::
1617	`buw-rpl` —
1618	List all {burp_regime} profiles for the current {burs_user}.
1619	
1620	[[bust_privilege_regime_render]]
1621	//axvo_tabtarget
1622	{bust_privilege_regime_render}::
1623	`buw-rpr` —
1624	Render a single {burp_regime} profile
1625	({burp_investiture} passed as positional argument).
1626	
1627	[[bust_privilege_regime_validate]]
1628	//axvo_tabtarget
1629	{bust_privilege_regime_validate}::
1630	`buw-rpv` —
1631	Validate a single {burp_regime} profile
1632	({burp_investiture} passed as positional argument).
1633	
1634	==== Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only)
1635	
1636	[[bust_fenestrate]]
1637	//axvo_tabtarget
1638	{bust_fenestrate}::
1639	`buw-jpF` —
1640	{busn_fenestrate} ceremony: hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config`
1641	on a Windows OpenSSH node (uniform; no shell-letter variant).
1642	Capital `F` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.
1643	
1644	==== Privileged SSH
1645	
1646	[[bust_privileged_ssh]]
1647	//axvo_tabtarget
1648	{bust_privileged_ssh}::
1649	`buw-jpS` —
1650	Thin admin-side pass-through that runs an arbitrary command on a
1651	node as {burp_privileged_user} under key-only auth.
1652	Pass-through: argv after the {burp_investiture} is handed to ssh
1653	as the remote command without shell wrapping; the operator
1654	prepends `powershell -Command`, `bash -c`, or whatever the
1655	target platform requires.
1656	Scope spans every {burn_platform} — Linux/Mac admin trust comes
1657	from operator-managed `ssh-copy-id`, Windows admin trust from
1658	{busn_fenestrate}.
1659	Capital `S` marks the admin-side scope (privileged user) and
1660	distinguishes it from `buw-jws` (workload interactive session).
1661	
1662	==== Garrison (per shell-letter)
1663	
1664	[[bust_garrison_bash]]
1665	//axvo_tabtarget
1666	{bust_garrison_bash}::
1667	`buw-jpGb` —
1668	{busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell
1669	(Linux and macOS nodes).
1670	Capital `G` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.
1671	
1672	[[bust_garrison_cygwin]]
1673	//axvo_tabtarget
1674	{bust_garrison_cygwin}::
1675	`buw-jpGc` —
1676	{busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell
1677	(Windows nodes via Cygwin sshd).
1678	
1679	[[bust_garrison_wsl]]
1680	//axvo_tabtarget
1681	{bust_garrison_wsl}::
1682	`buw-jpGw` —
1683	{busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell
1684	(Windows nodes via WSL distribution `rbtww-main`).
1685	
1686	==== Workload Dispatch
1687	
1688	[[bust_knock]]
1689	//axvo_tabtarget
1690	{bust_knock}::
1691	`buw-jwk` —
1692	Probe workload reachability via key-only SSH;
1693	returns 0 if the workload account answers a no-op command.
1694	
1695	[[bust_command_file]]
1696	//axvo_tabtarget
1697	{bust_command_file}::
1698	`buw-jwc` —
1699	Run the contents of a local command file as the workload account;
1700	shell is determined by whichever shell-letter garrisoned the node.
1701	Captures stdout, stderr, and remote exit status to {burd_output_dir}.
1702	
1703	[[bust_interactive_session]]
1704	//axvo_tabtarget
1705	{bust_interactive_session}::
1706	`buw-jws` —
1707	Open an interactive SSH session to the workload account.
1708	
1709	==== Handbook
1710	
1711	[[bust_handbook_jurisdiction_top]]
1712	//axvo_tabtarget
1713	{bust_handbook_jurisdiction_top}::
1714	`buw-hj0` —
1715	Top-level handbook landing for jurisdiction procedures
1716	(sshd-reachability bootstrap, fenestrate and garrison hand-off,
1717	dispatch usage).
1718	
1719	=== Verb Definitions
1720	
1721	[[busn_fenestrate]]
1722	//axl_voices axt_command
1723	{busn_fenestrate}::
1724	Establish admin SSH key trust and harden Windows OpenSSH
1725	`sshd_config` to key-only on a node, leaving it ready for
1726	{busn_garrison}.
1727	Uniform across shell-letters — one tabtarget (`buw-jpF`).
1728	Targets Windows OpenSSH only; Linux/Mac sshd hardening is
1729	operator-manual outside BUK.
1730	Idempotent on re-run.
1731	+
1732	Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` terminating its
1733	own ssh session:
1734	+
1735	* Phase 1: SSH as
1736	{burp_privileged_user}
1737	with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` (one-time password
1738	fallback on a fresh node);
1739	idempotently place the admin pubkey
1740	(derived from
1741	{burp_privileged_key_file}
1742	via `ssh-keygen -y`)
1743	in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry (no `command=`);
1744	apply `icacls` lockdown;
1745	write the hardening directives to `sshd_config`
1746	(`PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
1747	`PasswordAuthentication no`,
1748	`PermitEmptyPasswords no`);
1749	verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` + bash-side
1750	parsing;
1751	run `sshd -t` as the penultimate atomic op; restart sshd as the
1752	final atomic op.
1753	* Phase 2: reconnect using
1754	{burp_privileged_key_file}
1755	with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback);
1756	verify the post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.
1757	+
1758	The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately after
1759	`Restart-Service sshd` as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
1760	
1761	[[busn_garrison]]
1762	//axl_voices axt_command
1763	{busn_garrison}::
1764	Provision or refresh the workload account on a node from an existing
1765	admin SSH foothold.
1766	A single tabtarget per shell-letter
1767	(`buw-jpGb`, `buw-jpGc`, `buw-jpGw`)
1768	runs the same destructive ceremony with the shell-letter selecting
1769	remote shell mappings via
1770	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
1771	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never alters
1772	admin authorized_keys; first-step admin SSH must succeed by key auth
1773	alone, surfacing any missing admin trust immediately.
1774	Idempotent on re-run; destructive of any prior workload state.
1775	+
1776	Ceremony steps:
1777	+
1778	. SSH as
1779	{burp_privileged_user}
1780	using
1781	{burp_privileged_key_file};
1782	key-auth only (no password fallback). On Windows, this presupposes
1783	that
1784	{busn_fenestrate}
1785	has run for this {burp_investiture}.
1786	. Destroy any existing workload account named
1787	{burc_workload_user}
1788	via `userdel -r` or platform equivalent — removes account and home
1789	directory.
1790	. Create fresh workload account named
1791	{burc_workload_user}:
1792	unprivileged, no sudo, no admin group, ssh-only.
1793	. Place workload pubkey
1794	(derived from
1795	{burp_workload_key_file}
1796	via `ssh-keygen -y`)
1797	in workload's authorized_keys with the shell-letter's `command=`
1798	directive.
1799	. Copy
1800	{burp_workload_key_file}
1801	to the remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
1802	{bujb_jurisdiction}
1803	per shell-letter,
1804	so the workload can authenticate outbound (e.g., to GitHub).
1805	. Validate round-trip: SSH as workload, exec a no-op, verify exit 0.
1806	
1807	=== Input Artifacts
1808	
1809	[[busi_scribed_command_file]]
1810	//axl_voices axo_entity
1811	{busi_scribed_command_file}::
1812	A file authored by the operator containing commands to be executed
1813	on the remote node by {bust_command_file}.
1814	Path passed as a positional argument; contents are streamed
1815	to the remote shell at dispatch time.
1816	
1817	include::BUSTLL-NodeRegimeList.adoc[]
1818	
1819	include::BUSTLR-NodeRegimeRender.adoc[]
1820	
1821	include::BUSTLV-NodeRegimeValidate.adoc[]
1822	
1823	include::BUSTPL-PrivilegeRegimeList.adoc[]
1824

[[bust_privilege_regime_list]]

{bust_privilege_regime_list}::
`buw-rpl` —
List all {burp_regime} profiles for the current {burs_user}.

[[bust_privilege_regime_render]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_render}::
`buw-rpr` —
Render a single {burp_regime} profile
({burp_investiture} passed as positional argument).

[[bust_privilege_regime_validate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_validate}::
`buw-rpv` —
Validate a single {burp_regime} profile
({burp_investiture} passed as positional argument).

==== Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only)

[[bust_fenestrate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_fenestrate}::
`buw-jpF` —
{busn_fenestrate} ceremony: hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config`
on a Windows OpenSSH node (uniform; no shell-letter variant).
Capital `F` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.

==== Privileged SSH

[[bust_privileged_ssh]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privileged_ssh}::
`buw-jpS` —
Thin admin-side pass-through that runs an arbitrary command on a
node as {burp_privileged_user} under key-only auth.
Pass-through: argv after the {burp_investiture} is handed to ssh
as the remote command without shell wrapping; the operator
prepends `powershell -Command`, `bash -c`, or whatever the
target platform requires.
Scope spans every {burn_platform} — Linux/Mac admin trust comes
from operator-managed `ssh-copy-id`, Windows admin trust from
{busn_fenestrate}.
Capital `S` marks the admin-side scope (privileged user) and
distinguishes it from `buw-jws` (workload interactive session).

==== Garrison (per shell-letter)

[[bust_garrison_bash]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_bash}::
`buw-jpGb` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell
(Linux and macOS nodes).
Capital `G` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.

[[bust_garrison_cygwin]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_cygwin}::
`buw-jpGc` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell
(Windows nodes via Cygwin sshd).

[[bust_garrison_wsl]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_wsl}::
`buw-jpGw` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell
(Windows nodes via WSL distribution `rbtww-main`).

==== Workload Dispatch

[[bust_knock]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_knock}::
`buw-jwk` —
Probe workload reachability via key-only SSH;
returns 0 if the workload account answers a no-op command.

[[bust_command_file]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_command_file}::
`buw-jwc` —
Run the contents of a local command file as the workload account;
shell is determined by whichever shell-letter garrisoned the node.
Captures stdout, stderr, and remote exit status to {burd_output_dir}.

[[bust_interactive_session]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_interactive_session}::
`buw-jws` —
Open an interactive SSH session to the workload account.

==== Handbook

[[bust_handbook_jurisdiction_top]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_handbook_jurisdiction_top}::
`buw-hj0` —
Top-level handbook landing for jurisdiction procedures
(sshd-reachability bootstrap, fenestrate and garrison hand-off,
dispatch usage).

=== Verb Definitions

[[busn_fenestrate]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_fenestrate}::
Establish admin SSH key trust and harden Windows OpenSSH
`sshd_config` to key-only on a node, leaving it ready for
{busn_garrison}.
Uniform across shell-letters — one tabtarget (`buw-jpF`).
Targets Windows OpenSSH only; Linux/Mac sshd hardening is
operator-manual outside BUK.
Idempotent on re-run.
+
Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` terminating its
own ssh session:
+
* Phase 1: SSH as
{burp_privileged_user}
with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` (one-time password
fallback on a fresh node);
idempotently place the admin pubkey
(derived from
{burp_privileged_key_file}
via `ssh-keygen -y`)
in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry (no `command=`);
apply `icacls` lockdown;
write the hardening directives to `sshd_config`
(`PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
`PasswordAuthentication no`,
`PermitEmptyPasswords no`);
verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` + bash-side
parsing;
run `sshd -t` as the penultimate atomic op; restart sshd as the
final atomic op.
* Phase 2: reconnect using
{burp_privileged_key_file}
with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback);
verify the post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.
+
The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately after
`Restart-Service sshd` as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.

[[busn_garrison]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_garrison}::
Provision or refresh the workload account on a node from an existing
admin SSH foothold.
A single tabtarget per shell-letter
(`buw-jpGb`, `buw-jpGc`, `buw-jpGw`)
runs the same destructive ceremony with the shell-letter selecting
remote shell mappings via
{bujb_jurisdiction}.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never alters
admin authorized_keys; first-step admin SSH must succeed by key auth
alone, surfacing any missing admin trust immediately.
Idempotent on re-run; destructive of any prior workload state.
+
Ceremony steps:
+
. SSH as
{burp_privileged_user}
using
{burp_privileged_key_file};
key-auth only (no password fallback). On Windows, this presupposes
that
{busn_fenestrate}
has run for this {burp_investiture}.
. Destroy any existing workload account named
{burc_workload_user}
via `userdel -r` or platform equivalent — removes account and home
directory.
. Create fresh workload account named
{burc_workload_user}:
unprivileged, no sudo, no admin group, ssh-only.
. Place workload pubkey
(derived from
{burp_workload_key_file}
via `ssh-keygen -y`)
in workload's authorized_keys with the shell-letter's `command=`
directive.
. Copy
{burp_workload_key_file}
to the remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction}
per shell-letter,
so the workload can authenticate outbound (e.g., to GitHub).
. Validate round-trip: SSH as workload, exec a no-op, verify exit 0.

=== Input Artifacts

[[busi_scribed_command_file]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busi_scribed_command_file}::
A file authored by the operator containing commands to be executed
on the remote node by {bust_command_file}.
Path passed as a positional argument; contents are streamed
to the remote shell at dispatch time.

include::BUSTLV-NodeRegimeValidate.adoc[]

include::BUSTPL-PrivilegeRegimeList.adoc[]

content.text:
I have all I need. Let me lay out the plan briefly before executing — wanted to confirm the obliterate mechanism before committing to it.

The docket says the obliterate helper is letter-agnostic and platform-aware, with Windows scope spanning Windows SAM + `C:\Users\<user>\` + `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` + WSL `/home/<user>` inside `rbtww-main`. The natural Windows admin shell for crossing all four namespaces is PowerShell (already present via `zbujb_admin_powershell`) — it can call `net.exe`, remove Windows paths, and dispatch into WSL via `wsl.exe`. Bash via Cygwin bash or WSL bash can't easily reach all four. Linux/Mac uses bash via `zbujb_admin_exec b`. Going with PowerShell-on-Windows + bash-on-Linux-or-Mac unless you prefer different.

questions.question: Obliterate helper transport on Windows nodes: which shell?

options.label: PowerShell (Recommended)
options.description: Use zbujb_admin_powershell on Windows. Crosses all four namespaces (SAM via net.exe, Windows profile, Cygwin home, WSL-side userdel via wsl.exe) in one ssh connection. Symmetric with bujb_wsl_install.

options.label: Cygwin bash
options.description: Reach Windows from Cygwin bash via cygdrive paths + net.exe + wsl.exe. Awkward (cygdrive vs C:\); requires Cygwin sshd present even for w-letter garrison.

options.label: WSL bash
options.description: Reach Windows from WSL bash via /mnt/c/ + cmd.exe shims. Also awkward; couples obliterate to rbtww-main being installed even for c-letter garrison.

**Edit 1: Step 2 destroy → `zbujb_obliterate_workload` (platform-dispatched, letter-agnostic)**

input.old_string:
# Step 2 -- destroy any existing workload account + home.
zbujb_garrison_step2_destroy() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step2: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
  buc_step "  [2/6] Destroy workload (${z_wlu})"

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
sudo -n userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo -n rm -rf '${z_wlhome}' 2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    c)
      # Cygwin workload is a Windows local user; delete via net.exe then
      # purge the Cygwin home directory.
      zbujb_admin_exec c <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
rm -rf '${z_wlhome}'                              2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf '/cygdrive/c/cygwin64/home/${z_wlu}'       2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    w)
      # Workload identity is mirrored across two namespaces: a Windows user
      # (SSH auth boundary) and a Linux user inside the WSL distro
      # (execution context). Purge both, idempotent.
      zbujb_admin_exec w <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
userdel -r '${z_wlu}'                           2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf '${z_wlhome}'                            2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
  esac
}
input.new_string:
# zbujb_obliterate_workload -- letter-agnostic, platform-dispatched destroy
# helper. Single greppable verb covering every namespace a prior garrison
# of any letter could have populated. Idempotent on absent state at every
# step; (letter, platform) compatibility is asserted by garrison entry, so
# the helper dispatches on BURN_PLATFORM alone.
#
# Windows scope (one PowerShell session over admin SSH):
#   - Windows SAM entry (net.exe user /delete)
#   - C:\Users\<user>\ Windows profile directory
#   - C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\ Cygwin home
#   - WSL-side /home/<user> via wsl.exe userdel -r inside rbtww-main
# Linux scope: native userdel -r and home purge.
# Mac scope: sysadminctl -deleteUser and home purge.
zbujb_obliterate_workload() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  buc_step "  [2/6] Obliterate workload (${z_wlu}) on ${BURN_PLATFORM}"

  case "${BURN_PLATFORM}" in
    bubep_linux)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
sudo -n userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo -n rm -rf '/home/${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    bubep_mac)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
sudo -n sysadminctl -deleteUser '${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo -n rm -rf '/Users/${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    bubep_windows)
      # \$global:LASTEXITCODE is reset after each native-command probe so
      # absent-state non-zero codes do not leak to the wrapper's trailing
      # exit-code check. WSL block runs only if rbtww-main is installed
      # (probed via wsl.exe --list --quiet).
      local -r z_obliterate_body="& net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete 2>&1 | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (Test-Path 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; if (Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; \$wslList = wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (\$wslList -match '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}') { wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>&1 | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0 }"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "${z_obliterate_body}" \
        || buc_die "Obliterate failed on ${BURN_HOST}"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "obliterate: unsupported BURN_PLATFORM '${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
      ;;
  esac
}

toolUseResult.oldString:
# Step 4 -- write workload authorized_keys with the shell-letter command=
# directive and the workload pubkey (derived locally from the privkey).
zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
  buc_step "  [4/6] Place workload trust (${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys)"

  local z_command_directive
  z_command_directive=$(bujb_command_for_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: bujb_command_for_capture failed for letter='${z_letter}'"

  ssh-keygen -y -P '' -f "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
      > "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}" \
      2> "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}" \
    || buc_die "ssh-keygen -y failed for workload key: ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE} — see ${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}"
  local z_pubkey
  z_pubkey=$(<"${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}")
  z_pubkey="${z_pubkey//$'\n'/}"

  local z_authkeys_line="${z_command_directive} ${z_pubkey}"

  # Precompute sudo prefix locally (bash parameter expansion, no $()) so the
  # heredoc lines don't carry inline `$([ ... ] && echo "sudo -n ")` calls.
  local z_sudo_prefix=""
  test "${z_letter}" = "b" && z_sudo_prefix="sudo -n "

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
${z_sudo_prefix}mkdir -p   '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 700  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' | ${z_sudo_prefix}tee '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys' > /dev/null
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 600  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
${z_sudo_prefix}chown -R '${z_wlu}:${z_wlu}' '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    c)
      # In Cygwin, file ownership/permissions interplay with NTFS ACLs.
      # mkdir/chmod/chown are Cygwin-mediated; run as the admin user
      # without sudo (Windows admins already have privilege).
      zbujb_admin_exec c <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
chmod 700  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chown -R '${z_wlu}' '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    w)
      # Authorized_keys lives Windows-side so Windows OpenSSH discovers
      # it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows user). From
      # WSL bash the Windows profile is reachable at /mnt/c/Users.
      # Structural shape mirrors the c branch above; the path differs.
      zbujb_admin_exec w <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
chmod 700  '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chown -R '${z_wlu}' '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
  esac
}
toolUseResult.newString:
# Step 4 -- write workload authorized_keys with the shell-letter command=
# directive and the workload pubkey (derived locally from the privkey).
zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"

  # Authorized_keys directory differs by letter: b/c land in the workload
  # home; w lands Windows-side under /mnt/c/Users so Windows OpenSSH
  # discovers it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows user).
  local z_authkeys_dir
  case "${z_letter}" in
    b|c) z_authkeys_dir="${z_wlhome}/.ssh" ;;
    w)   z_authkeys_dir="/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh" ;;
  esac
  buc_step "  [4/6] Place workload trust (${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys)"

  local z_command_directive
  z_command_directive=$(bujb_command_for_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: bujb_command_for_capture failed for letter='${z_letter}'"

  ssh-keygen -y -P '' -f "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
      > "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}" \
      2> "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}" \
    || buc_die "ssh-keygen -y failed for workload key: ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE} — see ${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}"
  local z_pubkey
  z_pubkey=$(<"${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}")
  z_pubkey="${z_pubkey//$'\n'/}"

  local z_authkeys_line="${z_command_directive} ${z_pubkey}"

  local z_sudo_prefix=""
  test "${z_letter}" != "b" || z_sudo_prefix="sudo -n "

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
${z_sudo_prefix}mkdir -p   '${z_authkeys_dir}'
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 700  '${z_authkeys_dir}'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' | ${z_sudo_prefix}tee '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys' > /dev/null
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 600  '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys'
${z_sudo_prefix}chown -R '${z_wlu}:${z_wlu}' '${z_authkeys_dir}'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    c)
      # In Cygwin, file ownership/permissions interplay with NTFS ACLs.
      # mkdir/chmod/chown are Cygwin-mediated; run as the admin user
      # without sudo (Windows admins already have privilege).
      zbujb_admin_exec c <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '${z_authkeys_dir}'
chmod 700  '${z_authkeys_dir}'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys'
chown -R '${z_wlu}' '${z_authkeys_dir}'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    w)
      # No chown: POSIX ownership on /mnt/c/... is meaningless to Windows
      # OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs. Inherited NTFS ACL from the
      # parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only for
      # non-admins — satisfies StrictModes. Explicit icacls hardening is
      # deferred unless a real host kicks back.
      zbujb_admin_exec w <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '${z_authkeys_dir}'
chmod 700  '${z_authkeys_dir}'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '${z_authkeys_dir}/authorized_keys'
SCRIPT
      ;;
  esac
}

# Step 4 -- write workload authorized_keys with the shell-letter command=
# directive and the workload pubkey (derived locally from the privkey).
zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
  buc_step "  [4/6] Place workload trust (${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys)"

  local z_command_directive
  z_command_directive=$(bujb_command_for_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step4: bujb_command_for_capture failed for letter='${z_letter}'"

  ssh-keygen -y -P '' -f "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
      > "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}" \
      2> "${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}" \
    || buc_die "ssh-keygen -y failed for workload key: ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE} — see ${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK}"
  local z_pubkey
  z_pubkey=$(<"${ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK}")
  z_pubkey="${z_pubkey//$'\n'/}"

  local z_authkeys_line="${z_command_directive} ${z_pubkey}"

  # Precompute sudo prefix locally (bash parameter expansion, no $()) so the
  # heredoc lines don't carry inline `$([ ... ] && echo "sudo -n ")` calls.
  local z_sudo_prefix=""
  test "${z_letter}" = "b" && z_sudo_prefix="sudo -n "

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
${z_sudo_prefix}mkdir -p   '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 700  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' | ${z_sudo_prefix}tee '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys' > /dev/null
${z_sudo_prefix}chmod 600  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
${z_sudo_prefix}chown -R '${z_wlu}:${z_wlu}' '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    c)
      # In Cygwin, file ownership/permissions interplay with NTFS ACLs.
      # mkdir/chmod/chown are Cygwin-mediated; run as the admin user
      # without sudo (Windows admins already have privilege).
      zbujb_admin_exec c <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
chmod 700  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '${z_wlhome}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chown -R '${z_wlu}' '${z_wlhome}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
    w)
      # Authorized_keys lives Windows-side so Windows OpenSSH discovers
      # it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows user). From
      # WSL bash the Windows profile is reachable at /mnt/c/Users.
      # Structural shape mirrors the c branch above; the path differs.
      zbujb_admin_exec w <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p   '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
chmod 700  '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
echo '${z_authkeys_line}' > '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chmod 600  '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh/authorized_keys'
chown -R '${z_wlu}' '/mnt/c/Users/${z_wlu}/.ssh'
SCRIPT
      ;;
  esac
}

file.content:
== Remote Node Access

BUK reaches remote nodes under the
{bus_jurisdiction}
umbrella: a single station user establishes admin SSH trust on the
node, provisions one workload account, and dispatches commands to it.

[[bus_jurisdiction]]
{bus_jurisdiction}::
The BUK feature-area for reaching remote nodes deterministically.
Two verbs cover the privileged-side ceremonies:
{busn_fenestrate}
hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config` on Windows OpenSSH nodes
(operator-manual on Linux/Mac, e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); {busn_garrison}
provisions and refreshes a workload account from an existing admin
SSH foothold. The workload-side tabtargets
({bust_knock}, {bust_command_file}, {bust_interactive_session})
dispatch work to that account.
+
{bus_jurisdiction}
is logical only — there is no AXLA-typed entity behind the umbrella.
Its implementation seat is
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

[[bujb_jurisdiction]]
{bujb_jurisdiction}::
The BCG-compliant module
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` plus its `bujb_cli.sh` companion)
that houses both
{busn_fenestrate}
and
{busn_garrison}
implementations and hardcodes the
{bus_jurisdiction}
contracts not expressed in regime data:
the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings
(`b` = native bash for Linux/Mac;
`c` = Cygwin;
`w` = WSL)
used by garrison on the workload account,
the workload private-key destination paths on the remote per shell-letter,
the canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`),
and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by
fenestrate.
+
SSH transport mechanics live in code;
{burp_regime}
carries no shell selection.

=== Privileges

A
{burn_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/<{burn_investiture}>/` and is shared
across station users (git-transported); it carries node shape only and
no key material.
A
{burp_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/`;
the
{burs_user}
owns its credential subtree exclusively, and operations write only
under the current station user's tree.

{busn_garrison}
authenticates as
{burp_privileged_user}
using
{burp_privileged_key_file}
by key alone; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on Windows (which uses a one-time password fallback on its own
phase-1 admin SSH session) or operator-manually on Linux/Mac.
Workload account is unprivileged, ssh-only, and named by
{burc_workload_user}
(project-wide convention, single source of truth).

=== Garrison-Destructive Model

//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_bash}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
for Linux and macOS nodes.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{burc_workload_user}
and recreates it from a clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac this
admin trust is operator-managed (e.g., placed via `ssh-copy-id`) —
there is no `busn_fenestrate` for non-Windows-OpenSSH nodes.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} account
via `userdel -r`.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
ssh-only account.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
+
[listing]
----
command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload account named
{burc_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
failure, or workload validation failure.

Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
and recreates it from a clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
On success the node has exactly one workload account named
-{burc_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
+{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.

//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_cygwin}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via Cygwin sshd.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{burc_workload_user}
and recreates it from a clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while Cygwin sshd hardening (if used
in addition to Windows OpenSSH) remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} (Windows OpenSSH path). Cygwin sshd is
installed on the node.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} Cygwin
account via the Cygwin user-management equivalent of `userdel -r`.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
ssh-only Cygwin account.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
the shell-letter `c` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_c` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
+

----

toolUseResult.originalFile:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
On Windows the workload identity is *mirrored across two namespaces*:
a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
WSL execution context entered by the forced command.
The Windows-user shape mirrors the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin});
the WSL-side `useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as
the named WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's
default user.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{burc_workload_user} in *both* namespaces and recreates it from a
clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user}
identity in *both* namespaces — the Windows user via
`net.exe user /delete` and the WSL Linux user via `userdel -r`.
Both purges are idempotent.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} in *both*
namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
/active:yes` (mirroring the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side
authorized_keys at `C:\Users\${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}\.ssh\authorized_keys`
(reachable from WSL as `/mnt/c/Users/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/...`)
so Windows OpenSSH discovers it natively. The c branch is the
structural reference for mkdir/chmod/echo/chmod/chown ordering.
The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` is substituted with the
configured workload user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{burc_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
workload validation failure.

Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named

for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).

user is the same shape minted by the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin}),
so Windows OpenSSH treats inbound auth identically; the WSL-side
`useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as the named

{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
single greppable destroy verb. The covenant scope spans every
namespace a prior garrison of any letter could have written to: the
Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\` Windows profile directory,
the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home, and the WSL-side
`/home/<user>` inside `rbtww-main`. Each step is idempotent on
absent state.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
On Windows the workload identity is *mirrored across two namespaces*:
a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
WSL execution context entered by the forced command.
The Windows-user shape mirrors the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin});
the WSL-side `useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as
the named WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's
default user.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user} in *both* namespaces and recreates it from a
clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {bujb_workload_user}
identity in *both* namespaces — the Windows user via
`net.exe user /delete` and the WSL Linux user via `userdel -r`.
Both purges are idempotent.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} in *both*
namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
/active:yes` (mirroring the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side
authorized_keys at `C:\Users\${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}\.ssh\authorized_keys`
(reachable from WSL as `/mnt/c/Users/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/...`)
so Windows OpenSSH discovers it natively. The c branch is the
structural reference for mkdir/chmod/echo/chmod/chown ordering.
The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` is substituted with the
configured workload user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
workload validation failure.

input.new_string:
//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
(`C:\Users\<user>\`), the Cygwin home (`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`),
and the WSL-side Linux user inside `rbtww-main`
(`wsl.exe --user root userdel -r`).
Each operation is idempotent on absent state.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} in *both*
namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
/active:yes` (matching the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side authorized_keys at
`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
OpenSSH discovers it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows
user). The directory is created with `mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the
file is written and chmod 600. No POSIX `chown` is performed —
`/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless to Windows OpenSSH, which
inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS ACL from the parent profile
directory is admin-owned and read-only for non-admins, satisfying
StrictModes.
The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the
canonical workload user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
for Linux and macOS nodes.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic

toolUseResult.originalFile:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_bash}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
for Linux and macOS nodes.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user}
and recreates it from a clean slate.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac this
admin trust is operator-managed (e.g., placed via `ssh-copy-id`) —
there is no `busn_fenestrate` for non-Windows-OpenSSH nodes.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {bujb_workload_user} account
via `userdel -r`.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
ssh-only account.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
+
[listing]
----
command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload account named
{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
failure, or workload validation failure.

in addition to Windows OpenSSH) remains operator-manual.

file.content:
Provision or refresh the workload account on a node from an existing
admin SSH foothold.
A single tabtarget per shell-letter
(`buw-jpGb`, `buw-jpGc`, `buw-jpGw`)
runs the same destructive ceremony with the shell-letter selecting
remote shell mappings via
{bujb_jurisdiction}.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never alters
admin authorized_keys; first-step admin SSH must succeed by key auth
alone, surfacing any missing admin trust immediately.
Idempotent on re-run; destructive of any prior workload state.
+
Ceremony steps:
+
. SSH as
{burp_privileged_user}
using
{burp_privileged_key_file};
key-auth only (no password fallback). On Windows, this presupposes
that
{busn_fenestrate}
has run for this {burp_investiture}.
. Destroy any existing workload account named
{bujb_workload_user}
via `userdel -r` or platform equivalent — removes account and home
directory.
. Create fresh workload account named
{bujb_workload_user}:
unprivileged, no sudo, no admin group, ssh-only.
. Place workload pubkey
(derived from
{burp_workload_key_file}
via `ssh-keygen -y`)
in workload's authorized_keys with the shell-letter's `command=`
directive.

via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` — the letter-agnostic, platform-aware
destroy helper. Scope spans every namespace a prior garrison of any
letter could have written to (on Windows: SAM entry, Windows profile
directory, Cygwin home, WSL-side Linux user inside `rbtww-main`; on
Linux/Mac: native account + home). Idempotent on absent state.

On Linux: sudo userdel -r + rm -rf /home/<user>; on Mac: sudo sysadminctl -deleteUser + rm -rf /Users/<user>. Each step idempotent on absent state. (letter, BURN_PLATFORM) compatibility check stays at garrison entry (zbujb_garrison_assert_platform); helper dispatches on BURN_PLATFORM alone. bujb_garrison call site loses the letter argument to step 2. content.text: The obliterate helper hit a PowerShell-specific gotcha: `2>&1 | Out-Null` with `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` (set by the wrapper) promotes the merged stderr line ("The user name could not be found.") to a terminating error before our `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` reset can run. The fix: use `*>$null` (discard all streams at the IO layer) for the absent-tolerant native-command deletes — stderr never becomes a PS error stream entry, so `Stop` doesn't trip.

      # (probed via wsl.exe --list --quiet).
      local -r z_obliterate_body="& net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete 2>&1 | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (Test-Path 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; if (Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; \$wslList = wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (\$wslList -match '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}') { wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>&1 | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0 }"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "${z_obliterate_body}" \
        || buc_die "Obliterate failed on ${BURN_HOST}"
      ;;

content.text: `*>$null` didn't help — PowerShell still promotes the stderr line to a terminating error before the merge-and-discard happens. The reliable idiom is `2>$null` (discard stderr at IO before any error-stream conversion), which already worked for the `wsl --list --quiet` probe in the same body.

input.old_string:
bubep_windows)
      # Absent-tolerant native deletes use *>\$null (discard all streams at
      # the IO layer) rather than 2>&1: under the wrapper's
      # \$ErrorActionPreference='Stop', merging stderr into the success

      # is reset after each absent-state probe so non-zero codes do not
      # leak to the wrapper's trailing exit-code check. WSL block runs
      # only if rbtww-main is installed (probed via wsl.exe --list).
      local -r z_obliterate_body="& net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete *>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (Test-Path 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; if (Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; \$wslList = wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (\$wslList -match '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}') { wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root userdel -r '${z_wlu}' *>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0 }"

input.new_string:

      # Absent-tolerant native deletes use 2>\$null (stderr discarded at

content.text:
Stopping per your instruction. Three cycles ran:

1. `2>&1 | Out-Null` (merge stderr to success stream then discard) — failed
2. `*>$null` (redirect all streams to null) — failed
3. `2>$null | Out-Null` (discard stderr at IO before merge) — failed

Same error in all three at the column-arrow position right at `& net.exe user '...'`:

```
net.exe : The user name could not be found.
+ CategoryInfo : NotSpecified: (... :String) [], RemoteException
+ FullyQualifiedErrorId : NativeCommandError
```

**Diagnosis:** PowerShell 5.1 with `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` (set by the wrapper `zbujb_admin_powershell`) promotes any non-empty native-command stderr line into a terminating error **at the point of emission**, before the redirection on the same line takes effect. The redirect operator (`2>$null`, `*>$null`, `2>&1`) only chooses where the line *goes after* PS already saw it. There's no IO-level redirect that beats this — it's a PowerShell engine behavior, not a stream-routing one.

This contradicts what I assumed when I picked the design. `bujb_wsl_install` works only because `wsl.exe --list --quiet` happens to emit nothing to stderr on this host's state — not because its redirect pattern is doing protective work.

**Fix options for next cycle:**

- **A. Per-call `try { ... } catch { }`** — explicit absent-tolerance wrapper around each native call that may emit stderr. Clearest intent, doesn't weaken error handling for the rest of the body. ~6 char overhead per call.
- **B. Lower `$ErrorActionPreference` to `Continue` for just the obliterate body** — keep `Stop` for `zbujb_admin_powershell` callers in general, override at the body's first statement. Less surgical but smaller change.
- **C. Push stderr to OS via `cmd /c "... 2>nul"`** — cmd.exe swallows stderr before PS sees it. Awkward shape, mixes shells.
- **D. Probe-first via PS cmdlets** — `Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` for SAM, then `Remove-LocalUser`. Doesn't help the WSL `userdel -r` step (no PS cmdlet for "user inside WSL"), so still needs A or B for that.

Recommended: **A** — local, explicit, idiomatic PowerShell, doesn't compound abstractions. WSL userdel gets the same try/catch wrap.

Which option, or different direction?

message.content: I strongly urge trying to do less in each powershell line- your skills with powershell are shaky.  Can you make this simpler by splitting commands into smaller steps?

content.text:
Plan for next cycle — split into 4 independent PowerShell calls, each doing one thing, each its own SSH session:

| Sub-step | PowerShell body | Native cmds |
|---|---|---|
| `[2a/6] SAM entry` | `if (Get-LocalUser -Name '<u>' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) { Remove-LocalUser -Name '<u>' }` | none — pure PS cmdlets |
| `[2b/6] Windows profile dir` | `if (Test-Path 'C:\Users\<u>') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\Users\<u>' }` | none — pure PS cmdlets |
| `[2c/6] Cygwin home` | `if (Test-Path 'C:\cygwin64\home\<u>') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\cygwin64\home\<u>' }` | none — pure PS cmdlets |
| `[2d/6] WSL-side user` | `if ((wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>$null) -match 'rbtww-main') { try { wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root userdel -r '<u>' 2>$null \| Out-Null } catch { } }` | wsl.exe — wrapped in try/catch |

Three of the four sub-steps drop native commands entirely — pure PS cmdlets, no Stop-escalation risk to reason about. Only `wsl.exe userdel -r` (no PS cmdlet equivalent for "user inside WSL distribution") needs the try/catch wrap, and it's the only piece of fragile syntax in the new shape.

Bash side: 4 sequential `zbujb_admin_powershell` calls with `|| buc_die` per sub-step — failure localizes to the specific namespace. Adds ~3 SSH round-trips of latency to obliterate (one already exists), acceptable for a once-per-garrison destructive op.

Approve to proceed?

message.content: Actually, can we even simplify further?  Instead of having the 'if' statement inside of powershell, can't we just get the raw info to the bash script and let it do the 'if' statements?  Mostly its _die, right?

content.text:
You're right — pushing decision logic to bash is simpler. Plan for next cycle:

**Each PS call is one atom:**
- **Probes** emit raw text and exit. No `if`. No conditional logic.
  - SAM: `Get-LocalUser -Name '<u>' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` — emits object info (non-empty stdout) if present, empty if absent.
  - Profile/Cygwin paths: `Test-Path 'C:\Users\<u>'` — emits "True" or "False".
  - WSL distros: `wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>$null` — emits names.
  - WSL user: `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root getent passwd '<u>' 2>$null` — emits passwd line or empty.
- **Destructive calls** do one thing, unconditional from PS's view:
  - `Remove-LocalUser -Name '<u>'`
  - `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '<path>'`
  - `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root userdel -r '<u>'`

**Bash side does all gating:**
```
zbujb_admin_powershell "Test-Path 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}'" > "${ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT}" 2> "${ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDERR}" \
  || buc_die "Profile-dir probe failed — see ${ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDERR}"
z_state=$(<"${ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT}")
z_state="${z_state//[$'\r\n']/}"
test "${z_state}" != "True" || {
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}'" \
    || buc_die "Profile-dir removal failed"
}
```

PowerShell carries no conditional logic. Bash carries all of it via `test ... || { ... }` and `|| buc_die`. Up to 8 SSH calls (4 probes + up to 4 destructive); each fails atomically with a localized message. Adds 2 kindle temp slots (`ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT/STDERR`), reused across calls.

Approve this, or would you prefer something even leaner?

message.content: Approve bash doing all conditionals as long as it is BCG compliant.  You may proceed.

      # Absent-tolerant native deletes use 2>\$null (stderr discarded at
      # IO before any error-stream conversion). Under the wrapper's
      # \$ErrorActionPreference='Stop', any pattern that lets PowerShell
      # see the stderr line — including 2>&1 (merge to success stream)
      # and *>\$null (merge-then-redirect-all) — promotes native stderr
      # to a terminating error before our trailing \$LASTEXITCODE reset
      # can run. \$global:LASTEXITCODE is reset after each absent-state
      # probe so non-zero codes do not leak to the wrapper's trailing
      # exit-code check. WSL block runs only if rbtww-main is installed
      # (probed via wsl.exe --list --quiet).
      local -r z_obliterate_body="& net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete 2>\$null | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (Test-Path 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\Users\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; if (Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${z_wlu}' -ErrorAction Stop }; \$wslList = wsl.exe --list --quiet 2>\$null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0; if (\$wslList -match '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}') { wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>\$null | Out-Null; \$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0 }"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "${z_obliterate_body}" \
        || buc_die "Obliterate failed on ${BURN_HOST}"
      ;;
